speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 8, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Eli Sommer. The interviewer's name is Sylvia Messeri. The interview is being conducted in Fairlawn, New Jersey in the United States of America. And it is being conducted in English. My name is Sylvia Messeri, M-E-S-S-E-R-I. Today is November 8, 1996. I am conducting an interview with Eli Sommer. The interview is being conducted in Fairlawn, New Jersey in the United States of America, in English. What is your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eli Sommer. E-L-I S-O-M-M-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth? And spell it, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Elieser Sommer. E-L-I-E-S-E-R, Sommer, S-O-M-M-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born April 2, 1919. In--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, I am 67.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Poland, P-O-L-A-N-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tarnow. T-A-R-N-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Sommer, where is Tarnow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tarnow is southern Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And before the war, belonged to Austria and Hungary empire. But I was born right after Polish independence.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of town was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a town about between 55,000, 60,000 people with a big Jewish population. I would say about 40% of Jews were in-- were in Tarnow, about 25,000 before the war. And during the war even more, because they brought a lot of people to Tarnow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war, what did the Jews do there? What was the type of work that they did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, the Jewish population was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] they were workers. A lot of-- a lot of tailors, because it was confection-- I don't know how you-- confection is in English or not. And-- and there were a lot people who were working in this industry. Beside that, there were a lot of people who in all kind of work-- had all kind of work, lot of merchants, a lot of all kind of profession. There were doctors and-- and dentists. And but mostly, I would say, mostly workers. And big percentage was the tailors and the-- and the industry, confection industry. There were a few factories in this industry. And Tarnow was known in Poland as a-- as a big, good, good ready-wear-suits.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your father? What was his name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was Chaim Ayosef Sommer. He was born 19-- 1882. And he was a merchant. And actually, my whole family, as far as I remember, as far as I know, was in this-- this business for quite a long time. And it was a whole-sale flour business. We were supplying the bakers with flour. And it was pretty good business. I remember when I was a kid before the depression, we used to rent some mills and with partners. And we used to sell the flours to our store, to the bakers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father, was he a religious man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He was Orthodox man. He was not-- he was not, like, that he would-- he was Zionist religious. He belonged to-- to-- what is the? I forgot the Zionist Organization, Zionist Religious Organization. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but he was very religious. He was [NON-ENGLISH]. That means he was who was running with another elected officials to shul that he belonged to. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: The shul was [INAUDIBLE]. I was on [INAUDIBLE] Tarnow. And it was pretty good size of a shul. It was, I would say, a few hundred of people, families belonged there. It was not like shul here. It was not rich, but people were coming everyday to shul and especially on-- on Saturday and holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was in-- taking care of the family. She was a housewife, as you would call here. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Sedonia Chaindl And her maiden name was [INAUDIBLE]. And it was a pretty good marriage as far as I know. I never saw anything which discords or-- or loud voice in our family. And that's what I can say about my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she religious also?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- she was religious, because my father was more religious. That's my impression. Because as-- as far as I know, she was always going to the shul when the holidays were on. But I-- I think my father was more religious. And naturally, being a wife of a religious man, she was religious. She had a kosher house. That goes without saying. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she wear a wig?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No wig. We were not that religious. I mean, we were Orthodox, but not-- no wigs.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your mother's hobbies?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess her family was her hobbies. She lived for-- for the kids. And that's main thing what she did. Later on, when the kids were grown-- and I was the youngest in the family. And I was a teenager already. Being that she didn't have special-- anything specific to do, she liked to come to the stores and be involved a little bit in the business. But that was-- we had a help in the house. So she was not tied down to the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that usual to have help in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: The help was very cheap before the war in Poland. And you didn't have to be very rich to-- to have help. And it was a help that-- live-in help. She was sleeping in-- and-- I-- it-- it depends what circles. In our circles, it was not unusual. Naturally, there were much-- there were a lot  of people who were very poor. And they didn't have help. And-- but I don't think it was that unusual, because it was very, very cheap. And usually, even people who were not well-to-do, being that the peasants were so poor, they were happy to have a house to live in and food to have on the table. That's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she cooked. With-- and the help cook helped her. But mainly, she was always preparing. She was doing the shopping, and she was preparing. She was involved always in the house. That was her hobby, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't know. I-- in our house, everybody had to finish whatever was put on the table. It was not like this, I don't like here. And I don't like this. And I don't like that. And I remember my mother used to-- always used to say that in her family, everybody is eating everything put on the table.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one sister, which was-- she was the oldest one. And I had two old--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Minka. And then I have two older brothers. One was Moses, [? Monek, ?] and one was [? Benek, ?] Baruch. And naturally, was a big difference in-- being that I was the youngest one, it was a big difference in-- in the year-- in the age. My-- my sister was 11 years older than I was. My brother was seven and a half years older, and one five years older. So I was the youngest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to a private school. It was a school which was formed about two years before I-- my-- I was going-- before I started school. And my brothers didn't-- my other brothers didn't go to this school. It was a Hebrew school where they taught the regular-- regular public Polish curriculum. On top of this, you had to have the Hebrew history and Hebrew language and Hebrew-- and Hebrew religion. So we had about six hours every day school. And it was six times a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your favorite subject?

speaker: interviewee
text: History. History was always my favorite subject. And I'm still very interested in history and politics and what takes the world goes-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the current history I like, too. So that was my favorite things.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: After school, we used to play soccer. That's what-- we called it football. And I was very interested in all kind of sports. I was playing ping pong. And I was playing soccer. And I was playing all kind of sports, actually, whatever I could, didn't cost too much money. Because my father was not very-- he-- he allowed me to do everything. But he wouldn't encourage me to do. Because he always thought that I should learn more and I put more time for books and-- and all kind things that with use more-- more head than sports. And being that he was Orthodox, actually, he-- he never went for sports. It's not like today that they take people, kids are going, father-- father, son going with the kids to play soccer, encourage to play all kind of sports. We had to do it on our own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you listen to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not always, no. When it comes to these kind of things, I always did whatever I wanted. And-- I remember when my-- I was pretty wild kid, too, when I was young. And I remember when my-- I had the family in the United States. My mother-- my mother had brothers here. And-- and they came in 1928 to visit. And they were ashamed. I was so wild. And I was always-- and my mother was ashamed that she didn't brought me right. But I guess that was my time-- time of life that I was wild. And once I grew up a little bit, then I changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other activities did you participate in?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was 12, I got involved in the-- in the Zionist movement. I belonged to [NON-ENGLISH], which was middle of the road Zionist, general Zionist. And-- and I was involved very much. We had a group of boys and girls that we used to meet every day. And we used to have all kind of-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we went through the history of Zionism. And we had all kind of discussions, and all kind of-- of-- it was involvement in Zionist movement. That's-- that's main thing is. And we had a good time, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you plan to go to Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. When I was a little bit older, around 16, everybody was trying. First of all, in Poland was a big-- it was anti-Semitic country. And it was not a future for one-- for a man who went to school, finished school. And for instance, when my brother was studying later, he couldn't-- when he finished, he started medicine in France. He couldn't come to-- to Poland and start his own-- to work in his profession. I have cousins who were doctors and were engineers. And they finished, and they worked. One doctor was-- one cousin was my-- was a doctor. And he was working for a director of hospital. But he couldn't-- being that he was Jewish, he couldn't make the-- the examination in English-- in Polish, because on the account that he was Jewish. They wouldn't let him take the examination. And without an examination, he couldn't open an office.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he had to struggle. He made-- he was a doctor after quite a few years after the-- after finish school. And he made a living by making injections. And he couldn't make a living on it. But that-- that was the way it was. And so on the question that you asked me, if-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the question was if I wanted to go to Israel? Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Palestine. Everybody wanted to get out. Being that I was a Zionist-- Zionist, naturally, that was my-- my goal, to go to Israel and at that time, Palestine. And-- and the only way to get out was to do the matura, and to-- because after the matura, I could go to the-- apply to Jerusalem, for instance, University. At that time I was a member, because my middle brother applied later on. And he went to Israel at the Jerusalem University. Which he didn't, actually, but he went out. He applied. He was accepted. And this way, he went to Palestine at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was our goal. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that's the only way. Because, otherwise, you had to go to the army. You got-- once you got in the army, it was-- I don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was three years when I'm not mistaken. Three years-- I don't remember exactly. And so everybody wanted to get out before that time if you could.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned ping pong. How much did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had-- we had a-- a sports club, Samson. And somehow we were very good in it, in ping pong. And we-- I have a very good friend. Actually, he belonged to the same [INAUDIBLE], same organization, together. And he was a champion of Poland in ping pong. And he was very good tennis players-- tennis player, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schift. He was-- and he-- he went. He was a champion in Poland. And then we have quite a few good players. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you on a team? A ping pong team?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were on a ping pong-- were on a team. And we were champion of Poland, actually, for quite a few years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play against only other Jews? Or, you played--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No. That was general, general. We played all kind of teams, Polish team. And we were-- we were champion of Poland. And this [? Milek ?] Schift, he was a champion for quite a few years, through the war, actually.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have Polish friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many. Not too many Polish. Somehow I was always involved in the Jewish organization. And in Poland, it was very hard to have actually Jewish-- Polish friends. We did-- in commercial-- commercial life, we were involved, you know, in business with all kind of Polish people. But being that it was sort of very anti-Semitic [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] condition, usually Jews kept separately and-- and the Poles kept separately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you face any anti-Semitic incidences before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But I wouldn't say-- I wouldn't say that I was-- being that I-- I am not involved with them-- with Poles, I didn't have too many-- too many occasion to be with them. But you could-- during the-- whenever you met, you could feel and you could notice certain things. But I, personally, never had any-- any-- any-- time-- any opportunities to see it, I mean to-- by hitting or something, no. There were some-- sometimes when we were playing soccer games, for instance, or kind of games and sports games, there were very occasion that, you know, they were hitting, they were fighting, and, you know. But-- but this I wouldn't say had to be-- you could see the anti-Semitism in this. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: How? How?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were calling names. And they were hitting. They were always fighting. They were always hitting, when-- when-- especially when Jewish team played against the other team, the Polish team. There were always-- if somebody, a Jewish would win, they were starting some kind of fights always. Always were hitting, they're always fighting. With-- it usually started with name-calling, and then they started fight somehow.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in out sport-- in ping pong, I didn't have that. You could-- because we were always so much better that nobody could-- could-- it was no contest even. So I couldn't see it even, in this way. What was your favorite holiday?

speaker: interviewee
text: My favorite holiday was holiday to-- to be out of school, I guess. That was-- [LAUGHS] I don't know. I always find the-- the holidays, I guess Sukkot was very nice holiday, happy holidays, usually. I-- I never liked to be too much in-- in-- in synagogue praying all day. So the less I had to go to pray, the better I felt, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you spend your holidays with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always with the family. Always in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- my sister was married when I was 12. So she was always coming with-- then she had a family. She used to come to-- to our house. So my brothers were not home, as far as I remember later. Because one-- one was in Palestine. And one was in France. And but we used to visit. My father had-- had some brothers and sisters in town, so used to visit. And they used to come. And we had family from Krakow. They used to come also, sometimes for the holidays. But that's [INAUDIBLE] many, the family-- the very close family that we have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your grandparents alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- no, I don't remember. I-- I remember my father's grandmother-- my father's mother. But I remember her when she was already in bed. And they were always telling-- they were telling stories how smart she was when she was-- she was not sick, that she was a business woman. She was running the-- the stores. And but at that time she was-- they called it-- I don't know whether it was Alzheimer's. But she was in the 60s. But she was considered in 60s very old. And I remember she was always in bed. And they were saying that she had [NON-ENGLISH]. That means the artery closed up. And-- and she died when she was in the 60s. And my-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember, my grandfather, my father's father. He was dead when I was born. And I-- I remember my mother's father. He was alive. And he was married second time. And he had-- he lived in different town. And he was married to another-- to-- to-- his wife was also a widow. And she had children from the first husband. But we didn't see each other that often. Because at that time, even that she-- he was living about, I would say, 80 kilometer, which would be 50 miles. But that was a big distance in Poland. You had to take a train and ride quite a-- a few hours. But once I spent a holidays-- during the summer, I went for-- for to visit him. And that was about two weeks. And then my first-- the grandmother, my mother's mother, the-- the first wife of my grandfather, was dead when I was born.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your parents were born in Tarnow, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was born in Tarnow. My-- my mother was born in Jaslo which was a town I don't know exactly how far. I would say about 40 kilometers or something from Tarnow. But also-- but she, then her father moved to Nowy Sacz That's what she lived. She was born in Jaslo. But she actually lived in Nowy Sacz I understand, which was about 80 kilometer from Tarnow.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.
